verses.text: Après une corégence avec son père Jotham, Achaz devint roi à part entière et régna pendant seize ans, de 731 à 715 av. J.-C.

28:2 He walked in the ways of the kings of Israel, and made also molten images for Baalim: Ahaz's idolatry is identified by its northern model — the kings of Israel rather than the kings of David. The Chronicler names Baal-worship by Ahaz the southern king, a syncretism that would invite the prophetic verdicts of Isaiah and Micah (cp. Isa 7:1-14; Mic 1:5).
et il fit passer ses fils par le feu : Achaz imitait les pratiques abominables des Cananéens (voir **Dt 12.31** ; **18.9-10** ; **Jr 7.31** ; **19.5** ; **32.35** ).
et il fit passer ses fils par le feu : Achaz imitait les pratiques abominables des Cananéens (voir **Dt 12.31** ; **18.9-10** ; **Jr 7.31** ; **19.5** ; **32.35** ).

28:5 the Lord . . . delivered him into the hand of the king of Syria . . . he was also delivered into the hand of the King of Israel—These verses, without alluding to the formation of a confederacy between the Syrian and Israelitish kings to invade the kingdom of Judah, or relating the commencement of the war in the close of Jotham's reign (Kg2 15:37), give the issue only of some battles that were fought in the early part of the campaign.

28:6 A hundred and twenty thousand - It is very probable that there is a mistake in this number. It is hardly possible that a hundred and twenty thousand men could have been slain in one day; yet all the versions and MSS. agree in this number. The whole people seem to have been given up into the hands of their enemies.

28:7 Maaseiah the king's son—the sons of Ahaz being too young to take part in a battle, this individual must have been a younger son of the late King Jotham; Azrikam the governor of the house—that is, "the palace"; and Elkanah that was next to the king—that is, the vizier or prime minister (Gen 41:40; Est 10:3). These were all cut down on the field by Zichri, an Israelitish warrior, or as some think, ordered to be put to death after the battle.

28:8 the children of Israel carried away captive of their brethren two hundred thousand—These captives included a great number of women, boys, and girls, a circumstance which creates a presumption that the Hebrews, like other Orientals, were accompanied in the war by multitudes of non-combatants (see on Jdg 4:8). The report of these "brethren," being brought as captives to Samaria, excited general indignation among the better-disposed inhabitants; and Oded, a prophet, accompanied by the princes (2 Chr 28:12 compared with 2 Chr 28:14), went out, as the escort was approaching, to prevent the disgraceful outrage of introducing such prisoners into the city.

et vous les avez tués avec une fureur qui est montée jusqu’aux cieux : ils étaient allés trop loin. L'Éternel avait utilisé Israël comme moyen de punition envers Juda, mais l'alliance interdisait l'asservissement et le meurtre de ses compatriotes israélites ( **28.10** ; **Lv 25.39-55** ).

et vous les avez tués avec une fureur qui est montée jusqu’aux cieux : ils étaient allés trop loin. L'Éternel avait utilisé Israël comme moyen de punition envers Juda, mais l'alliance interdisait l'asservissement et le meurtre de ses compatriotes israélites ( **28.10** ; **Lv 25.39-55** ).

L'auteur du Livre des Chroniques adopte une attitude positive envers le royaume du Nord en utilisant le terme « frères ». • car la colère ardente de l’Éternel est sur vous : plus tard, sous le règne d'Achaz, le royaume d'Israël sera détruit et exilé (722 av. J.-C., **2R 17.5-23** ).

28:12 Then certain of the heads of the children of Ephraim,.... Of the principal men of the ten tribes, whose names follow: Azariah the son of Johanan, Berechiah the son of Meshillemoth, and Jehizkiah the son of Shallum, and Amasa the son of Hadlai, stood up against them that came from the war; who were bringing the captives and spoils to Samaria; but these princes, being influenced by what the prophet said, hindered their proceeding any further.

28:13 And said unto them, ye shall not bring in the captives hither,.... That is, into Samaria, near which it seems they now were: for whereas we have offended against the Lord already; by exceeding in their cruelty against their brethren of Judah, as well as by worshipping the calves: ye intend to add more to our sins and to our trespass: by making slaves of those they had taken captives, which was against the express law of God: for your trespass is great;

28:14 So the armed men left the captives and the spoil before the princes and the congregation. Which were come out of Samaria to meet them; such an effect had the word's of the prophet, and the princes, upon them, that they not only left the captives with them, but the spoil, to dispose of, as they thought fit. So the armed men left the captives and the spoil before the princes and the congregation. Which were come out of Samaria to meet them;

28:15 the men which were expressed by name rose up—These were either the "heads of the children of Ephraim" (mentioned 2 Chr 28:12), or some other leading individuals chosen for the benevolent office. Under their kindly superintendence, the prisoners were not only released, but out of the spoils were comfortably relieved with food and clothing, and conveyed as far as Jericho on their way back to their own homes. This is a beautiful incident, and full of interest, as showing that even at this period of national decline, there were not a few who steadfastly adhered to the law of God.
Achaz envoya demander du secours aux rois d’Assyrie : Voir **2R 16.7-9** pour plus de détails.

Les Édomites et les Philistins étaient des ennemis naturels de Juda. Les villes capturées par ces armées se situaient le long des vallées d'Ajalon, de Sorek et des Térébinthes, dans la zone tampon des contreforts de Juda ou du Néguev.

Les Édomites et les Philistins étaient des ennemis naturels de Juda. Les villes capturées par ces armées se situaient le long des vallées d'Ajalon, de Sorek et des Térébinthes, dans la zone tampon des contreforts de Juda ou du Néguev.

28:19 For Ahaz took away a portion out of the house of the Lord, and out of the house of the king, and of the princes,.... A part out of the treasures of the temple, and a part out of his own treasures, and another which he levied upon his nobles: and gave it unto the king of Assyria; sent it to him as a present, to engage him on his side, and assist him against his enemies, Kg2 16:10. but he helped him not;

28:20 Tilgath-pilneser . . . distressed him, but strengthened him not—that is, notwithstanding the temporary relief which Tilgath-pilneser afforded him by the conquest of Damascus and the slaughter of Rezin (Kg2 16:9), little advantage resulted from it, for Tilgath-pilneser spent the winter in voluptuous revelry at Damascus; and the connection formed with the Assyrian king was eventually a source of new and greater calamities and humiliation to the kingdom of Judah (2 Chr 28:2-3).

28:21 But he helped him not - He did him no ultimate service. See the note on Kg2 16:9. After Kg2 16:15, the 23d, 24th, and 25th verses are introduced before the 16th, in the Syriac and Arabic, and the 22d verse is wholly wanting in both, though some of the expressions may be found in the twenty-first verse.
Le roi Achaz a méprisé l'Éternel en faisant construire un autel inspiré de celui de Damas ( **2R 16.10-16** ) et en offrant des sacrifices aux dieux de Damas.
Le roi Achaz a méprisé l'Éternel en faisant construire un autel inspiré de celui de Damas ( **2R 16.10-16** ) et en offrant des sacrifices aux dieux de Damas.
Juda a atteint son point le plus bas sur le plan spirituel, une condition proche de celle de l'exil, sous le règne du roi Achaz.
Juda a atteint son point le plus bas sur le plan spirituel, une condition proche de celle de l'exil, sous le règne du roi Achaz.

Samarie, la capitale du royaume du nord d'Israël, est tombée aux mains de l'Assyrie en 722 av. J.-C., pendant la vingt et unième année du règne d'Achaz en Juda ( **2R 17.1-6** ). L'auteur du Livre des Chroniques, qui se concentre sur le royaume du sud, ne fait même pas mention de cet événement.

28:27 The kings of Israel - It is a common thing for the writer of this book to put Israel for Judah. He still considers them as one people, because proceeding from one stock. The versions and MSS. have the same reading with the Hebrew; the matter is of little importance, and with this interpretation none can mistake.
